statement.markdown: Given a triangle of numbers, find the maximum sum path from top to bottom. The path can be either directly below or diagonally below.

constraints.description: number of rows in the triangle
number of elements in each row
value of each element in the triangle

examples.explanation: The maximum sum path is 1 + 3 + 6 = 10 or 1 + 2 + 5 + 3 = 11

hints.text: The problem involves finding a maximum sum path in a triangle of numbers, which suggests using dynamic programming to store and reuse intermediate results.
The path can only go directly below or diagonally below, which means that for each element in the triangle, you only need to consider the maximum sum path from the two elements directly above it.

The problem can be solved by working from the second last row to the first row, updating each element with the maximum sum it can produce by considering the elements below it, effectively using a bottom-up dynamic programming approach.

editorial.title: Optimizing the Triangle Problem Solution
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved using dynamic programming. The key observation is that the maximum sum of the path from the top to the bottom of the triangle can be computed by iteratively updating the elements from the second last row to the first row.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve exploring all possible paths from the top to the bottom of the triangle, which would result in an exponential time complexity. This approach is impractical for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach involves using dynamic programming to store and reuse the maximum sum of the path for each sub-triangle.
editorial.optimalSolution: The dynamic programming approach modifies the input triangle in-place, starting from the second last row. For each element, it adds the maximum of the two elements directly below it. This process continues until the first row, which will hold the maximum sum of the path.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the dynamic programming approach can be proven by induction. The base case is the last row, where the maximum sum for each element is the element itself. For the inductive step, assume that the maximum sum for each element in the k-th row is correct. Then, for the (k-1)-th row, adding the maximum of the two elements directly below each element will give the maximum sum of the path for that element, thus proving the correctness for the (k-1)-th row.
editorial.dryRun: For a triangle with n rows, the time complexity is O(n^2) and the space complexity is O(n^2) as we are modifying the input triangle in-place.
commonMistakes: Not recognizing the problem as a dynamic programming problem.
Incorrectly implementing the dynamic programming approach, leading to incorrect results.
Not handling the base case correctly.
Not considering the space complexity of the approach.
Incorrectly assuming the problem can be solved using a greedy approach.
Not verifying the correctness of the approach using test cases.
